name: Bril
summary: Cornell CS 6120 teaching IR. Every Bril program is a JSON document — functions, basic blocks, and instructions encoded as nested objects. Demonstrates that the wire format itself can be the IR, with no separate textual or binary form.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Bril (Big Red Intermediate Language) is a research IR designed at Cornell for teaching compiler construction (CS 6120). Its claim to fame: the IR is JSON-shaped. Every Bril program is a JSON document with functions, basic blocks, and instructions encoded as nested objects. Tools, optimizers, and analyzers can be written in any language that can parse JSON.

Bril is intentionally minimal — fewer instructions than LLVM, no type system beyond a small set of primitives, no implicit conversions. The didactic point is that an IR doesn't need to be complex to support real optimizations (LICM, GVN, dead-code elimination, register allocation).

sections.title: JSON wire format as the IR
sections.content:
The JSON encoding makes Bril uniquely accessible for student projects: a 50-line Python script can read a Bril program, transform it, and write it back. As a representation, Bril shows that *the wire format itself can be the IR* — there is no separate textual form, no separate binary, just JSON.

This design choice is unusual: most IRs (LLVM IR, MLIR, Cranelift CLIF) maintain at least two representations (binary for tools, textual for humans), and the textual form is rarely directly editable. Bril collapses the distinction.

sections.title: Didactic role
sections.content:
Bril is pedagogically the inverse of LLVM IR: rather than a battle-tested industrial substrate that students must navigate to do anything, Bril is a deliberately stripped-down medium where every classic optimisation can be implemented in tens of lines.

The broader design lesson is that compiler-construction teaching benefits from an IR whose surface area is small enough that students can hold the whole language in their heads. JSON shape supports that goal — there is no parser to write, no binary format to learn, no textual surface to memorise.
